blog.heading: Blog
demo.title: See it in action
demo.subtitle: Watch Takt organize tabs automatically as you browse.
hero.lede: Auto-group tabs by URL rule. Find any tab with Alt+Shift+Space. Prevent duplicates, reclaim memory. All local — no account, no telemetry.
hero.subLede: Built by a security engineer who couldn't stop hoarding tabs.
hero.subCta: No account needed. Free to start.
hero.screenshotAlt: Chrome browser with tabs automatically organized into color-coded groups by Takt
mark.title: One extension. Every color Chrome gives you.
features.rules.title: Group by rule. Walk away.
features.rules.body: Define URL patterns — contains, domain, glob, or full regex. Takt watches every new tab and files it into the right Chrome group with the right color. Manual or automatic. Free tier includes 5 rules with basic matching. Premium unlocks unlimited rules, all match types, title matching, and compound AND/OR conditions.
features.quickSwitch.title: Find any tab. Instantly.
features.quickSwitch.body: Press Alt+Shift+Space anywhere in Chrome. A fuzzy-search overlay appears — type a few letters and jump without touching your mouse. Searches all open tabs by title and URL. Premium extends results to hibernated tabs and your full browsing history.
features.duplicates.title: No duplicates. No surprise.
features.duplicates.body: Open the same URL twice and Takt closes the copy, focuses the original, and shows a quiet toast. Five matching strategies handle query strings, fragments, and trailing slashes. Bypass anytime with Shift+click or the context menu. Premium feature.
features.resources.title: See what your tabs cost.
features.resources.body: Per-tab memory measurement. Per-group health scores from A to F. Network request counts. Staleness tracking for tabs you forgot about. Set memory budgets and Takt discards tabs that exceed them. All measured locally — no external services. Premium feature.
features.hibernation.title: Sleep tabs you don't need.
features.hibernation.body: Takt fully closes idle tabs and saves their state — URL, title, tab group, scroll position. Memory is reclaimed the moment the tab closes. Wake any tab with one click and it returns exactly where you left it. Manual hibernation is always free. Set idle thresholds, memory pressure limits, or a tab ceiling to hibernate automatically — Premium features.
gridCard.sectionLede: The details that make the difference.
gridCard.profiles.title: Profiles for every context
gridCard.profiles.body: Create named rule sets — Work, Personal, Research. Switch in the popup or schedule auto-switching by time of day. Premium.
gridCard.keyboard.title: Keyboard-first
gridCard.keyboard.body: Alt+Shift+G to organize. Alt+Shift+U to ungroup. Alt+Shift+C to collapse. No mouse needed.
gridCard.suggestions.title: Rules you didn't write
gridCard.suggestions.body: Takt watches your unmatched tabs. When a domain cluster appears, it suggests a rule. Accept or dismiss. Free.
gridCard.contextMenu.title: Right-click, done
gridCard.contextMenu.body: Right-click any tab and choose "Create rule from this tab." Takt prefills the URL pattern and group name. Premium.
gridCard.portable.title: Portable settings
gridCard.portable.body: Export rules, settings, and profiles as a single JSON file. Import on another machine. Premium.
gridCard.sessions.title: Save your workspace
gridCard.sessions.body: Snapshot all your tab groups into a named session. Restore later in a new window or replace the current one. Up to 50 sessions. Premium.
gridCard.sync.title: Sync across devices
gridCard.sync.body: Pick a folder watched by your cloud client (Google Drive, Dropbox, iCloud) or connect Google Drive directly for automatic background sync. Premium.
gridCard.groupActions.title: Act on whole groups
gridCard.groupActions.body: Close, bookmark, or move an entire tab group to a new window in one click. Available from the Options page or right-click menu. Premium.
howItWorks.headline: How it works.
howItWorks.step1.title: Install
howItWorks.step1.body: Add Takt from the Chrome Web Store. No account. No setup wizard. Takes five seconds.
howItWorks.step2.title: Write a rule
howItWorks.step2.body: Pick a URL pattern and a group color. Or right-click any tab and let Takt prefill it.
howItWorks.step3.title: Forget about it
howItWorks.step3.body: Enable auto-mode. Takt watches every new tab and groups it. You never touch it again.
pricing.headline: Start free. Upgrade when you need more.
pricing.free.title: Free
pricing.premium.title: Premium
privacy.headline: Local-first. No accounts. No telemetry.
privacy.body: All data stays in your browser. The only outbound call is license validation for paid features. Nothing else leaves.
cta.headline: Quiet your browser.
cta.footnote: Works with Chrome and Chromium-based browsers.
footer.product.heading: Product
footer.docs.heading: Documentation
footer.newsletter.heading: Stay updated
footer.newsletter.label: Email address
docs.sidebar.heading: Documentation
docs.rules.lead: Rules tell Takt how to organize your tabs. Each rule matches tabs by URL or title and assigns them to a named, colored tab group.
docs.rules.structure.heading: Rule structure
docs.rules.structure.liName: Name — a label for your reference (e.g., "GitHub repos")
docs.rules.structure.liConditions: Conditions — one or more patterns to match against tab URLs or titles
docs.rules.structure.liGroupName: Group name — the label Chrome displays on the tab group
docs.rules.structure.liColor: Color — one of Chrome's 8 tab group colors
docs.rules.structure.liEnabled: Enabled — toggle rules on/off without deleting them
docs.rules.structure.p2: Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom in priority order. The first matching rule wins — later rules are not checked.
docs.rules.matchTypes.heading: Match types
docs.rules.matchTypes.contains.heading: Contains
docs.rules.matchTypes.contains.desc: Case-insensitive substring match. The pattern can appear anywhere in the URL.
docs.rules.matchTypes.exact.heading: Exact
docs.rules.matchTypes.exact.desc: The full URL must match the pattern exactly (case-insensitive).
docs.rules.matchTypes.domain.heading: Domain
docs.rules.matchTypes.domain.desc: Matches the hostname or any subdomain. Useful for grouping all pages from a site regardless of path.
docs.rules.matchTypes.glob.heading: Glob
docs.rules.matchTypes.regex.heading: Regex
docs.rules.matchTypes.regex.desc: Full JavaScript regular expression. The pattern is anchored — it must match the entire URL (equivalent to wrapping in ^...$).
docs.rules.matchTypes.regex.callout: Regex patterns are powerful but can be tricky. Use the Rule Tester in the options page to verify your pattern matches the URLs you expect.
docs.rules.conditionFields.heading: Condition fields
docs.rules.conditionFields.intro: Each condition matches against one of two fields:
docs.rules.conditionFields.liTitle: Title — the page title shown in the tab
docs.rules.conditionFields.liCaseSensiti: Case sensitive — by default, matching is case-insensitive
docs.rules.compound.heading: Compound conditions
docs.rules.compound.callout: Premium feature. Free users are limited to a single condition per rule.
docs.rules.priority.heading: Rule priority
docs.rules.priority.p1: Rules are evaluated in the order they appear in your rule list. The first matching rule wins — the tab is assigned to that rule's group, and no further rules are checked.
docs.rules.priority.p2: Drag rules in the options page to reorder them. Put more specific rules higher, general catch-all rules lower.
docs.rules.colors.heading: Group colors
docs.rules.colors.p1: Chrome supports 8 tab group colors: grey, blue, red, yellow, green, pink, purple, and cyan.
docs.rules.colors.p2: All 8 colors are available to free and Premium users.
docs.smartGroupAi.overview.heading: Overview
docs.smartGroupAi.how.heading: How it works
docs.smartGroupAi.enable.heading: How to enable
docs.smartGroupAi.fallback.heading: Semantic fallback
docs.smartGroupAi.privacy.heading: Privacy
docs.smartGroupAi.gotchas.heading: Edge cases & gotchas
docs.autoCollapse.lead: Automatically collapse other tab groups when you expand one — keep your workspace focused without manual tidying.
docs.autoCollapse.overview.heading: Overview
docs.autoCollapse.overview.p1: Auto-collapse watches for tab group expansions and collapses competing groups automatically. The feature ships in two tiers: a same-window mode available to all users and a cross-window mode for Premium subscribers.
docs.autoCollapse.overview.p2: Both settings are off by default. You must opt in via Settings → TAB GROUPS. Each toggle starts disabled; you must turn it on manually.
docs.autoCollapse.overview.p3: The settings are global toggles — they apply across all profiles and all windows. There is no profile-specific auto-collapse override.
docs.autoCollapse.howItWorks.heading: How it works
docs.autoCollapse.howItWorks.p1: When auto-collapse is enabled, Takt listens for tabGroups.onUpdated events. Whenever a group transitions to expanded (collapsed: false) and it is the only expanded group in its window, Takt collapses all other groups in the same window. The predicate is stateless — no suppression flag is stored. This means:
docs.autoCollapse.howItWorks.liNoFeedbac: No feedback loop. Collapsing groups does not itself trigger further collapse events.
docs.autoCollapse.howItWorks.p2: Cross-window auto-collapse is triggered by tabs.onActivated events instead. When you switch to a tab in window A, Takt collapses all tab groups in every other open window after a debounce of 300 ms (DEBOUNCE_MS = 300 in src/shared/constants.js). The debounce prevents thrashing when switching tabs in quick succession.
docs.autoCollapse.tiers.heading: Free vs Premium
docs.autoCollapse.tiers.intro: Auto-collapse has two independent toggles, each controlling a different scope.
docs.autoCollapse.tiers.sameWindow: Enable same-window auto-collapse in Settings → TAB GROUPS → "Auto-collapse other groups when one expands".
docs.autoCollapse.tiers.crossWindow: Enable cross-window auto-collapse in Settings → TAB GROUPS → "Also collapse groups in other windows" (this toggle is disabled and labelled Premium for free users).
docs.autoCollapse.tiers.independent: The two settings are independent. A Premium subscriber with only same-window enabled gets same-window behavior only — enabling the Premium toggle is a separate opt-in.
docs.autoCollapse.gotchas.heading: Edge cases and gotchas
docs.autoCollapse.gotchas.li2: 300 ms debounce on cross-window. Brief tab-switches (under 300 ms) do not trigger cross-window collapse. This prevents a quick tab-select from collapsing all your groups in other windows before you settle on a tab.
docs.autoCollapse.gotchas.li4: Global setting, not profile-specific. The auto-collapse toggles apply globally across all profiles. Switching to a different Takt profile does not change whether auto-collapse is active.
docs.autoCollapse.gotchas.li5: Stateless predicate. Takt does not store a suppression flag to prevent a group from being re-collapsed. If you expand a group and another group is already open, auto-collapse fires every time.
docs.driveSync.lead: Keep your rules, profiles, and sessions in sync across all your devices — automatically, using Google Drive. Sign in once and Takt handles everything silently in the background.
docs.driveSync.premiumCallout: Premium feature. Drive Sync requires a Premium subscription.
docs.driveSync.howItWorks.heading: How it works
docs.driveSync.howItWorks.p1: Takt stores your data in Google Drive's hidden app folder — a private, per-app space that only Takt can access. It is completely separate from your Google Drive files and never appears in the Drive UI. Other apps and people cannot see it.
docs.driveSync.howItWorks.p2: After a one-time sign-in, Takt automatically syncs every 15 minutes in the background. You can also sync on demand or force-push and force-pull at any time. A smart direction check runs on every sync: Takt reads the remote timestamp and compares it to the local one, then pushes if your local data is newer or pulls if another device synced more recently.
docs.driveSync.comparison.heading: Drive Sync vs File Sync
docs.driveSync.comparison.conclusion: Drive Sync is the simpler choice for most users. File Sync is better if you want to inspect or edit the sync files directly, or use a cloud provider other than Google.
docs.driveSync.setup.heading: Setting up Drive Sync
docs.driveSync.setup.li1: Open the Takt Options page and go to the Settings tab.
docs.driveSync.setup.li2: Scroll to the Drive Sync section.
docs.driveSync.setup.li3: Click Connect Google Drive. A Google sign-in window will appear.
docs.driveSync.setup.li4: Sign in and grant Takt permission to access its hidden app folder. Takt requests the minimum possible scope — it cannot read or modify any of your regular Drive files.
docs.driveSync.setup.li5: Once connected, your account email appears next to the button. You can now sync.
docs.driveSync.setup.callout: The sign-in window appears only once. After that, Chrome caches the authentication token and renews it silently. You will not be prompted again unless you explicitly disconnect or revoke access from your Google account settings.
docs.driveSync.syncFiles.heading: What gets synced
docs.driveSync.syncFiles.intro: Takt stores four files in Google Drive's app folder:
docs.driveSync.syncFiles.conclusion: The files are written independently. A change to rules on device A and profiles on device B will not cause a conflict between those two files.
docs.driveSync.syncNow.heading: Sync Now
docs.driveSync.syncNow.p1: Click Sync Now to trigger an immediate sync. Takt compares timestamps and automatically pushes or pulls:
docs.driveSync.syncNow.liPush: Push — if your local data is newer than what is on Drive, your data is uploaded.
docs.driveSync.syncNow.liPull: Pull — if another device synced to Drive more recently, the remote data is downloaded and replaces your local data.
docs.driveSync.syncNow.p2: The Last synced timestamp updates when the operation completes successfully.
docs.driveSync.autoSync.heading: Auto-sync
docs.driveSync.autoSync.p1: Enable Auto-sync every 15 minutes to have Takt sync automatically in the background. The Settings panel shows a countdown to the next scheduled sync, refreshed every 30 seconds.
docs.driveSync.autoSync.p2: Auto-sync runs silently. Failures (for example, a temporary network error) are swallowed without any notification — the next scheduled sync will retry. If you need to ensure a sync succeeded, use Sync Now and check the Last synced timestamp.
docs.driveSync.forcePushPull.heading: Force Push and Force Pull
docs.driveSync.forcePushPull.intro: These operations bypass the automatic direction check and ignore timestamps entirely.
docs.driveSync.forcePushPull.liForcePush: Force Push — overwrites everything on Drive with your current local data. Use this to reset the remote state, for example after correcting corrupted data or when setting up Drive Sync for the first time on a device that already has your data.
docs.driveSync.forcePushPull.liForcePull: Force Pull — replaces your local data with everything currently on Drive. Use this to bring a new device fully up to date.
docs.driveSync.forcePushPull.callout: Force Push and Force Pull are destructive. Force Push overwrites Drive unconditionally. Force Pull replaces all local rules, profiles, and sessions with the remote copy. Both operations are immediate and cannot be undone from within Takt.
docs.driveSync.disconnect.heading: Disconnecting
docs.driveSync.disconnect.p1: Click Disconnect to sign out of Google Drive. Takt revokes its cached access token, clears your account email from Settings, and turns off auto-sync. Your data on Drive is not deleted — the files remain in the hidden app folder and can be accessed again if you reconnect.
docs.driveSync.disconnect.p2: To delete the data from Drive entirely, disconnect, then go to Google Account → Security → Third-party access and remove Takt's access there. This also removes the files from the hidden app folder.
docs.driveSync.privacySection.heading: Privacy
docs.driveSync.privacySection.intro: Takt requests two OAuth scopes from Google:
docs.driveSync.privacySection.liDriveFol: Drive app folder — read and write access to a hidden folder only Takt can see. Takt cannot read, list, or modify any file in your regular Google Drive.
docs.driveSync.privacySection.liEmail: Email address (read-only) — used only to display which account is connected in the Settings panel. Not stored on any server.
docs.driveSync.privacySection.p2: Your sync data is stored exclusively in your own Google account's Drive. Anthropic and the Takt developers have no access to it.
docs.driveSync.limits.heading: Limits & behaviour
docs.duplicates.lead: Stop opening the same tab twice. Takt detects duplicate URLs, closes the new tab, and focuses the original.
docs.duplicates.premiumCallout: Premium feature. Duplicate prevention requires a Premium subscription.
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.heading: How it works
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.intro: When you open a new tab:
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.li1: Takt normalizes the URL using your chosen strategy
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.li2: It checks the normalized URL against all currently open tabs
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.li3: If a match is found: the new tab is closed, the existing tab is focused, and a brief toast notification appears
docs.duplicates.howItWorks.li4: If no match: the tab opens normally
docs.duplicates.strategies.heading: URL normalization strategies
docs.duplicates.strategies.intro: Different strategies control how strictly URLs are compared. Set a global default in Settings, or override per rule.
docs.duplicates.strategies.conclusion: The default strategy is ignoreFragment, which works well for most sites.
docs.duplicates.universal.heading: Universal normalization
docs.duplicates.universal.intro: All strategies apply these normalizations before comparing:
docs.duplicates.perRule.heading: Per-rule strategy
docs.duplicates.perRule.intro: Each rule can override the global default. This is useful when different sites need different levels of strictness:
docs.duplicates.bypass.heading: Bypass methods
docs.duplicates.bypass.intro: When you intentionally want to open a duplicate:
docs.duplicates.bypass.liShift: Shift+click a link — the duplicate check is bypassed for that navigation
docs.duplicates.bypass.liContextMenu: Right-click → "Open in new tab (allow duplicate)" — context menu bypass
docs.duplicates.bypass.liOpenAnyway: "Open anyway" — click in the toast notification or popup after a duplicate is blocked
docs.duplicates.enable.heading: Enabling duplicate prevention
docs.duplicates.enable.li1: Open the Options page and go to Settings
docs.groupActions.lead: Act on entire tab groups at once — close, bookmark, or move them to a new window.
docs.groupActions.premiumCallout: Premium feature. Group Actions require a Premium subscription.
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.heading: Close Group
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.intro: Closes all tabs in a group at once.
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.liPinned: Pinned tabs are skipped. Pinned tabs in the group are left open; the count of skipped tabs is shown in the feedback toast.
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.liProtectio: Last-tab-in-window protection. If closing the group would leave a window completely empty (accounting for pinned tabs), Takt opens a blank new tab first so the window stays open.
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.liRegistry: The group is removed from Takt's registry after closing.
docs.groupActions.closeGroup.confirmatio: A confirmation prompt appears before closing, showing the number of tabs that will be closed.
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.heading: Bookmark Group
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.intro: Saves all tabs in the group as Chrome bookmarks inside a Takt Bookmarks folder.
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.liRoot: Root folder. Takt creates a single Takt Bookmarks folder in your Chrome bookmarks (under Other Bookmarks). If the folder already exists, it is reused.
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.liSubfol: Subfolder per save. Each bookmark action creates a timestamped subfolder if a subfolder with that group name already exists, so no bookmarks are overwritten.
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.liPermis: Bookmarks permission required. The Bookmarks permission must be granted in Settings → Permissions before this action is available. If the permission is missing, a prompt guides you there.
docs.groupActions.bookmarkGroup.readonly: The tabs themselves are not closed or moved — Bookmark Group is read-only.
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.heading: Move Group to New Window
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.intro: Detaches all tabs in the group into a brand-new Chrome window.
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.liProperties: Group properties preserved. The new window's group has the same title, color, and collapsed state as the original.
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.liConsolidat: Cross-window consolidation. If the group is spread across multiple windows (consolidated), all tabs are gathered into the single new window.
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.liProtection: Last-tab-in-window protection. Same as Close Group — if moving all group tabs would empty a source window, a blank tab is created first.
docs.groupActions.moveGroup.liRegistry: The registry is updated: the old group entries are removed and the new window's group is registered.
docs.groupActions.accessing.heading: Accessing Group Actions
docs.groupActions.accessing.optionsDesc: Open Options → Rules → Groups. Expand any group to see the Close, Bookmark, and Move to Window buttons. On a free plan, the buttons are visible but locked behind the Premium upgrade prompt.
docs.groupActions.accessing.contextDesc: Right-click any tab that belongs to a group. The Takt group actions submenu appears with three items corresponding to the three actions. If the tab is not in a group, a notification explains this. The submenu is only shown for Premium users.
docs.groupActions.limits.heading: Limits & behavior
docs.hibernation.lead: Fully close idle tabs to reclaim memory, then bring them back exactly where you left them — URL, scroll position, tab group, and pin state intact.
docs.hibernation.suspendVsHibernate.head: Suspension vs hibernation
docs.hibernation.suspendVsHibernate.intr: Takt manages tabs through a two-stage lifecycle:
docs.hibernation.suspendVsHibernate.conc: Suspension happens first. Hibernation is the deeper step: the tab is closed and Takt becomes responsible for its state. To get it back, you wake it from the popup or Options page.
docs.hibernation.whatIsSaved.heading: What gets saved
docs.hibernation.whatIsSaved.intro: When a tab is hibernated, Takt records:
docs.hibernation.whatIsSaved.storage: This record is stored in Chrome's local storage on your device. It is not synced across devices unless you use Drive Sync.
docs.hibernation.manual.heading: Manual hibernation
docs.hibernation.manual.intro: You can hibernate any tab on demand in three ways:
docs.hibernation.manual.liPopup: Popup — open the Takt popup, go to the Hibernate section, and click the hibernate icon next to any open tab.
docs.hibernation.manual.liContext: Context menu — right-click anywhere on a page and choose Hibernate this tab.
docs.hibernation.manual.liOptions: Options page — go to Options → Hibernate and click the hibernate button next to any tab in the list.
docs.hibernation.manual.free: Manual hibernation is free — no Premium subscription required.
docs.hibernation.exemptions.heading: Tabs that cannot be hibernated
docs.hibernation.exemptions.intro: Some tabs are always exempt from hibernation:
docs.hibernation.exemptions.li2: Pinned tabs — unless you enable Hibernate pinned tabs in Settings
docs.hibernation.exemptions.activeTab: The active tab in any window can be manually hibernated but is always skipped by automatic triggers.
docs.hibernation.waking.heading: Waking a tab
docs.hibernation.waking.li1: Open the Takt popup and go to the Hibernate section, or open Options → Hibernate
docs.hibernation.waking.li2: Click Wake next to the tab you want to restore
docs.hibernation.waking.p1: Takt reopens the tab in the same window it came from, at the same position, restores its tab group membership, and scrolls back to where you were.
docs.hibernation.waking.p2: If the same URL is already open in another tab, Takt will warn you before waking to avoid duplicates.
docs.hibernation.autoHibernation.heading: Auto-hibernation
docs.hibernation.autoHibernation.callout: Premium feature. All automatic hibernation triggers require a Premium subscription.
docs.hibernation.autoHibernation.intro: Auto-hibernation runs every 5 minutes and checks tabs against the rules you configure in Options → Settings → Tab Lifecycle.
docs.hibernation.idleThreshold.heading: Idle threshold
docs.hibernation.idleThreshold.p1: Enable Auto-hibernate suspended tabs after and set a duration. Any tab that has been suspended (discarded by Chrome) and idle for longer than that duration is automatically hibernated.
docs.hibernation.idleThreshold.p2: Idle time is measured from the last moment you interacted with the tab — clicked, scrolled, typed, or activated it. Tabs you have never interacted with since the extension was installed report an infinite idle time and are treated as the most stale candidates.
docs.hibernation.idleThreshold.callout: Auto-hibernation only targets tabs that are already suspended. It does not directly close active or loading tabs. If you want tabs to reach suspension first, enable Auto-suspend idle tabs in the same settings section.
docs.hibernation.perRule.heading: Per-rule hibernation threshold
docs.hibernation.perRule.p1: In Options → Rules, each rule can have its own hibernation threshold that overrides the global one. This lets you be aggressive with low-priority groups (e.g. hibernate news tabs after 1 hour) and conservative with high-priority ones (e.g. hibernate work tabs only after 3 days).
docs.hibernation.perRule.p2: A tab is hibernated if it meets either the global threshold or its rule-specific threshold — whichever fires first.
docs.hibernation.tabCeiling.heading: Tab ceiling
docs.hibernation.tabCeiling.p1: Enable Hibernate oldest tabs when a window exceeds N tabs and set a limit. Whenever a window goes over that number — including the moment you open a new tab — Takt hibernates the least-recently-used tabs until the count is back at the limit.
docs.hibernation.tabCeiling.p2: When the window drops below the limit (because you close a tab), Takt automatically restores the most recently hibernated tab back into that window, keeping the count at the ceiling.
docs.hibernation.tabCeiling.p3: The ceiling is enforced per window. Having 40 tabs split across two windows with a ceiling of 30 does not trigger hibernation; each window is evaluated independently.
docs.hibernation.schedule.heading: Schedule-end hibernation
docs.hibernation.schedule.p1: In Options → Profiles, each schedule can optionally hibernate all eligible tabs when the scheduled hours end. This is useful for work profiles: when your work hours finish, all open tabs are hibernated, and you start fresh next morning.
docs.hibernation.schedule.p2: Configure it by editing a profile's schedule and enabling Hibernate tabs when schedule ends.
docs.hibernation.undo.heading: Undoing auto-hibernation
docs.hibernation.undo.p1: Each time a tab is auto-hibernated, Takt shows a Chrome notification with an Undo button. Clicking Undo within 10 seconds immediately wakes the tab back. After 10 seconds, the undo window closes — but the tab remains in the Hibernate pane and can still be woken manually.
docs.hibernation.exemptPatterns.heading: Exempt URL patterns
docs.hibernation.exemptPatterns.p1: In Options → Settings → Tab Lifecycle, you can add URL patterns that should never be auto-hibernated. Patterns support * as a wildcard:
docs.hibernation.exemptPatterns.p2: Exempt patterns apply to all automatic triggers (idle threshold, ceiling, schedule). They do not prevent manual hibernation.
docs.hibernation.limits.conclusion: When the hibernation limit is reached, Takt blocks further hibernation and logs the reason. Wake or clear existing tabs to make room.
docs.hibernation.startup.p1: Hibernated tabs survive browser restarts — they are stored in Chrome's local storage, not in memory. When the browser starts, Takt checks whether any hibernated tab's URL is already open and removes the duplicate record if so. All other records remain and can be woken as normal.
docs.insights.lead: Takt watches your browsing patterns and suggests rules for domains you visit frequently — so you can build a rule set without starting from scratch.
docs.insights.overview.heading: Overview
docs.insights.overview.p1: The Insights pane in the popup surfaces auto-generated rule suggestions based on the domains you have open or visit often. Each suggestion card shows a domain that Takt has detected as a candidate for a new rule, along with a count of matching open tabs or history visits. Takt never stores your browsing history beyond the current session — analysis happens locally and on demand.
docs.insights.howItWorks.heading: How it works
docs.insights.howItWorks.intro: Takt generates suggestions from two sources:
docs.insights.howItWorks.liHistory: Browsing history — Takt scans your history for frequently-visited domains. A domain must meet either the minimum visit count or minimum active-days threshold to qualify. History-based results are capped at 20 per session.
docs.insights.howItWorks.p2: Domains already covered by an existing rule are excluded from suggestions — Takt won't suggest a rule you already have.
docs.insights.interactions.heading: Interactions
docs.insights.interactions.intro: Each insight card has two actions:
docs.insights.interactions.liCreateRule: Create Rule — opens the Options page with the domain pre-filled in the rule editor, so you can create a rule with one click. The card is removed after the rule is saved.
docs.insights.interactions.liDismiss: Dismiss — permanently hides the suggestion. The card won't reappear until your browsing patterns change significantly enough for the domain to re-qualify. Dismiss is persistent across popup opens within the session.
docs.insights.tiers.heading: Free vs Premium
docs.insights.tiers.p1: Free users see up to 3 insight cards at a time. If Takt has found more suggestions than the free limit, an upgrade prompt appears below the visible cards inviting you to unlock the hidden ones.
docs.insights.tiers.p2: Premium users see all suggestions with no cap. Upgrading removes the 3-card limit immediately — all pending suggestions become visible without reloading.
docs.insights.gotchas.heading: Edge cases and gotchas
docs.insights.gotchas.li1: Existing rules are excluded. If a domain is already covered by an existing rule, it won't appear as a suggestion. This prevents duplicate rules.
docs.insights.gotchas.li3: No insights. If Takt has no active suggestions (all dismissed, all covered by rules, or not enough tabs), the Insights pane shows an empty state with a message. This is normal — add more tabs or visit more domains to generate new suggestions.
docs.permissions.lead: Takt requests only the permissions it needs. Here's what each one does and why.
docs.permissions.required.heading: Required permissions
docs.permissions.required.intro: These are granted at install time and are necessary for core functionality:
docs.permissions.optional.heading: Optional permissions
docs.permissions.optional.intro: These are requested at runtime only when you enable features that need them:
docs.permissions.manage.heading: How to manage permissions
docs.permissions.manage.intro: Open the Options page and go to Settings. The permissions panel lets you:
docs.permissions.manage.liGrantAll: Grant All — grant all optional permissions at once
docs.permissions.manage.liIndividual: Individual toggles — grant or revoke each permission separately
docs.permissions.manage.liRevokeAll: Revoke All — revoke all optional permissions
docs.permissions.manage.liPromptMode: Prompt mode — choose whether Takt asks before requesting a permission ("Ask") or never requests automatically ("Never")
docs.permissions.manage.conclusion: Revoking a permission disables the feature that depends on it. The feature re-enables automatically when the permission is granted again.
docs.permissions.neverAccess.heading: What Takt never accesses
docs.permissions.neverAccess.li5: Analytics or telemetry of any kind
docs.permissions.neverAccess.privacyLink: For the full privacy story, see the Privacy Policy.
docs.profiles.lead: Save different rule sets as profiles and switch between them manually or on a schedule.
docs.profiles.premiumCallout: Premium feature. Profiles and scheduled switching require a Premium subscription.
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.heading: What are profiles
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.p1: A profile is a named subset of your rules. When a profile is active, only the rules assigned to it are evaluated. Rules not in any profile are always active ("All Rules" mode).
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.intro: Use profiles to separate different contexts:
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.liWork: Work — rules for Jira, GitHub, Confluence, Slack
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.liPersonal: Personal — rules for YouTube, Reddit, shopping sites
docs.profiles.whatAreProfiles.liResearch: Research — rules for academic papers, documentation sites
docs.profiles.creating.heading: Creating a profile
docs.profiles.creating.intro: Open the Options page and go to the Profiles tab:
docs.profiles.creating.li3: Choose to clone current rules (copies all rules into the new profile) or start empty
docs.profiles.creating.p2: New rules you create while a profile is active are automatically added to that profile.
docs.profiles.switching.heading: Switching profiles
docs.profiles.switching.p1: Click a profile card to activate it. Click "All Rules" to deactivate all profiles and use every rule.
docs.profiles.switching.li1: The rule engine reloads with only the active profile's rules
docs.profiles.switching.li2: Existing tab groups are not removed — only new organization actions use the updated rules
docs.profiles.switching.li3: Click "Group All" after switching to reorganize existing tabs
docs.profiles.schedules.heading: Scheduled switching
docs.profiles.schedules.intro: Automate profile switching by time of day and day of week:
docs.profiles.schedules.li1: On a profile card, click the schedule section
docs.profiles.schedules.li3: Set a start time and end time
docs.profiles.schedules.li4: Select which days of the week the schedule applies
docs.profiles.schedules.p1: When the schedule window begins, Takt activates the profile. When the window ends, it reverts to "All Rules" mode (unless you manually switched to a different profile).
docs.profiles.schedules.callout: Cross-midnight schedules are supported. A schedule from 10:00 PM to 6:00 AM activates at 10 PM and deactivates at 6 AM the next morning.
docs.quickSwitch.lead: Jump to any open tab instantly by typing part of its title or URL.
docs.quickSwitch.overview.heading: Overview
docs.quickSwitch.overview.p1: Quick Switch is a command-palette-style search overlay for your open tabs. Press the keyboard shortcut and start typing — Takt filters results in real time by tab title and URL, letting you navigate instantly without reaching for the mouse.
docs.quickSwitch.open.heading: Opening the overlay
docs.quickSwitch.open.p1: Quick Switch requires Premium. Press Alt+Shift+Space on any regular web page to open the overlay inline.
docs.quickSwitch.open.p2: On pages where content scripts cannot be injected, Takt opens a standalone popup window (560 × 480 px) instead. This fallback activates on URLs that match any of the following:
docs.quickSwitch.navigate.heading: Searching and navigating
docs.quickSwitch.navigate.p1: Type in the search box to filter results in real time. Filtering is case-insensitive and matches against the full tab title and full URL. Every keystroke updates the list immediately — including clearing the box, which returns to the full list.
docs.quickSwitch.ranking.heading: How results are ranked
docs.quickSwitch.ranking.intro: Results appear in four groups, always in this order:
docs.quickSwitch.ranking.liHibernated: Hibernated tabs — returned from the hibernation store in insertion order. Up to 50 hibernated tabs are shown.
docs.quickSwitch.ranking.p2: Takt does not apply any additional scoring or re-ordering beyond what Chrome provides. The combined list is always ordered: open tabs → hibernated tabs → history items → downloads.
docs.quickSwitch.ranking.liDownloads: Downloads — filenames of completed downloads, returned by chrome.downloads.search. Up to 20 downloads are shown, and only when the Downloads source is enabled.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.heading: Downloads search source
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.p1: Quick Switch can search the filenames of your completed downloads. It is a Premium feature and requires granting the optional Downloads permission.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.permission.p1: Grant the optional Downloads permission from Options → Settings → Permissions. The Downloads permission can be revoked; revoking it removes the Downloads source.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.privacy.p1: The search runs on-device via chrome.downloads.search and filenames never leave the browser.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.liFilenames: Only filenames are searched, not file contents.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.liQueryOnly: Downloads appear only when you are typing a query, not in the default unfiltered list.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.liOpenFile: Selecting a download result opens the file locally, not a browser tab.
docs.quickSwitch.downloads.liLimit: Up to 20 download results are shown, and only files that still exist on disk.
docs.quickSwitch.tiers.heading: Free vs Premium
docs.quickSwitch.tiers.p1: Quick Switch is a Premium feature. The keyboard shortcut Alt+Shift+Space is gated by canUseKeyboardShortcut(), which returns true only for Premium users. Free users pressing the shortcut see no response.
docs.quickSwitch.tiers.p2: For Premium users, Quick Switch searches open tabs and hibernated tabs by default. To also include browsing history results, grant the optional History permission from Options → Settings → Permissions. Without the History permission, Quick Switch is fully functional but no history results appear. The Downloads source is also Premium-gated and requires the optional Downloads permission (grant via Options → Settings → Permissions).
docs.quickSwitch.gotchas.heading: Edge cases and gotchas
docs.quickSwitch.gotchas.li2: No minimum query length. Results update on every keystroke. Clearing the search box returns the full open-tab list.
docs.quickSwitch.gotchas.li4: History results are deduplicated. URLs already present as open or hibernated tabs are excluded from the history section.
docs.quickSwitch.gotchas.li6: Zero open tabs. Opening Quick Switch when there are no open tabs shows "No open tabs" in the results area.
docs.quickSwitch.gotchas.li7: No matches found. If no results match your query, the results area shows "No matches found".
docs.resources.lead: See which tab groups consume the most memory. Get health scores, idle time tracking, and discard recommendations.
docs.resources.premiumCallout: Premium feature. The resource dashboard requires a Premium subscription.
docs.resources.overview.heading: Overview
docs.resources.overview.p1: The resource dashboard is in the Options page under the Dashboard tab. It shows real-time data about your tabs and groups, refreshing every 10 seconds while visible.
docs.resources.overview.intro: Four types of data are collected:
docs.resources.overview.liMemory: Memory — JavaScript heap size per tab
docs.resources.overview.liNetwork: Network — request counts, third-party domains, background activity
docs.resources.overview.liStaleness: Staleness — how long since you last interacted with a tab
docs.resources.overview.liDom: DOM complexity — number of DOM nodes in each page
docs.resources.healthScores.heading: Health scores
docs.resources.healthScores.intro: Each tab group receives a health score from A (healthy) to F (problematic), calculated from four factors:
docs.resources.healthScores.liA: A (90-100) — low resource use, recently active
docs.resources.healthScores.liD: D (40-59) — high use, likely candidates for cleanup
docs.resources.healthScores.liF: F (0-39) — excessive use, strongly recommend action
docs.resources.groupBreakdown.heading: Group breakdown
docs.resources.groupBreakdown.p1: The group breakdown shows a memory bar chart sorted by total memory. Each group card displays:
docs.resources.tabTable.heading: Tab table
docs.resources.tabTable.p1: Below the group breakdown, tabs are listed in collapsible group cards. Each tab shows:
docs.resources.tabTable.p2: Click any column header to sort. Use the search field to filter tabs by title or URL.
docs.resources.budgets.heading: Memory budgets
docs.resources.budgets.p1: Set per-group memory limits in Settings → Memory Budgets. When a group exceeds its budget, Takt shows a browser notification.
docs.resources.budgets.p2: Budgets are checked every 60 seconds while the extension is running. The dashboard also shows a visual indicator when a group is over budget.
docs.resources.discard.heading: Discard advisor
docs.resources.discard.intro: The discard advisor recommends tabs to close based on a weighted score:
docs.resources.discard.p2: The advisor excludes your active tab, pinned tabs, and tabs in groups with fewer than 2 tabs (to preserve the group).
docs.resources.discard.p3: Click "Suspend" to unload a tab from memory. The tab stays in your tab bar but releases its resources. Clicking it reloads the page.
docs.resources.discard.callout: Chrome still shows memory? After suspending a tab, Chrome's native tab hover card may still display a small memory footprint (typically 2–5 MB). This is normal — Chrome retains metadata for the tab's session history, favicon, and scroll position. The renderer process and JavaScript heap are fully released. Takt cannot control Chrome's native UI for this display.
docs.resources.permissions.heading: Required permissions
docs.resources.permissions.intro: The resource dashboard needs two optional permissions to show full data:
docs.resources.permissions.callout: Memory shows "—"? This means the all_urls permission is not granted. Takt injects a lightweight script into each tab to sample its memory — without host access, the measurement cannot run. Go to Settings → Permissions and grant All website access. Memory values will appear on the next dashboard refresh (within 10 seconds).
docs.sessions.lead: Snapshot your browser windows and restore them later — including tab groups, ungrouped tabs, and multiple windows.
docs.sessions.premiumCallout: Premium feature. Saving and restoring sessions requires a Premium subscription. You can browse saved sessions for free.
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.heading: What is a session
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.intro: A session is a named snapshot of one or more Chrome windows. Each session captures:
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.li1: Every open tab — URL, title, and pinned state
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.li2: Tab group names, colors, and collapsed states
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.li3: Which tabs belong to which group
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.li4: Ungrouped tabs (tabs not inside any group)
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.li5: Optionally, all open windows at once
docs.sessions.whatIsASession.storage: Sessions are stored locally in Chrome's storage and are not synced across devices.
docs.sessions.saving.heading: Saving a session
docs.sessions.saving.intro: Open the Takt popup and find the Sessions section:
docs.sessions.saving.li2: Enter a name (up to 60 characters). Each session name must be unique — if the name already exists, the save is blocked and the field stays open so you can choose a different name.
docs.sessions.saving.li3ThisWindow: This window — captures only the current Chrome window (default)
docs.sessions.saving.li3AllWindows: All windows — captures every open normal Chrome window
docs.sessions.saving.limit: You can save up to 50 sessions. Once the limit is reached, delete older sessions before saving new ones.
docs.sessions.saving.callout: Tabs on restricted Chrome pages (chrome://, about:, extension pages) are silently skipped when saving and restoring, since Chrome does not allow programmatic navigation to those URLs.
docs.sessions.restoring.heading: Restoring a session
docs.sessions.restoring.intro: Click Restore next to a session in the popup. A dialog appears with two options:
docs.sessions.restoring.liNewWindow: Open in new window — creates a fresh Chrome window and opens all saved tabs inside it. Your current window is untouched.
docs.sessions.restoring.liReplace: Replace this window — opens the saved tabs in the current window, then closes the tabs that were already there.
docs.sessions.restoring.countdown: Takt counts down 7 seconds before restoring automatically using the selected mode. Click Restore now to proceed immediately, or Cancel to abort.
docs.sessions.multiWindow.heading: Multi-window sessions
docs.sessions.multiWindow.intro: When you restore a session saved with All windows:
docs.sessions.multiWindow.li1: The first saved window uses your chosen mode (new or replace)
docs.sessions.multiWindow.li2: Each additional saved window always opens as a new Chrome window
docs.sessions.managing.heading: Managing sessions
docs.sessions.managing.popupIntro: The Sessions section in the popup shows your most recent sessions. Each entry displays:
docs.sessions.managing.optionsIntro: Open the Takt options page and go to the Sessions tab for a full detail view:
docs.sessions.exportImport.heading: Export & Import
docs.sessions.exportImport.p1: Sessions are included in Takt's full Export All / Import All bundle (available in Options → Settings). This lets you back up all your sessions along with rules and profiles.
docs.sessions.exportImport.p2: When importing, each session receives a fresh ID so it does not overwrite any existing session with the same name.
docs.sessions.limits.heading: Limits & behaviour
docs.shortcuts.lead: Organize your tabs without touching the mouse.
docs.shortcuts.default.heading: Default shortcuts
docs.shortcuts.default.p1: Organize, ungroup, and collapse shortcuts are available to all users. Quick-switch (Alt+Shift+Space) requires Premium.
docs.shortcuts.quickSwitch.heading: Quick-switch overlay
docs.shortcuts.quickSwitch.p1: The Quick Switch overlay is a command-palette style search for your open tabs. Press the shortcut and start typing — tabs are filtered in real time by title and URL. Use ↑↓ to navigate, Enter to jump to the selected tab, Tab to autocomplete, and Esc to dismiss.
docs.shortcuts.quickSwitch.p2: Premium expands the results to include hibernated tabs (shown with an ice-blue treatment and moon icon) and browsing history (shown in orange). Selecting a hibernated tab wakes it automatically. Selecting a history result opens that URL in a new tab.
docs.shortcuts.quickSwitch.p3: On pages where content scripts cannot be injected (e.g. chrome:// pages), a popup window opens instead.
docs.shortcuts.quickSwitch.linkText: See the Quick Switch page for full documentation on keyboard interactions, result ranking, and edge cases.
docs.shortcuts.customize.heading: Customizing shortcuts
docs.shortcuts.customize.intro: Chrome lets you change extension keyboard shortcuts:
docs.shortcuts.customize.li3: Click the pencil icon next to the shortcut you want to change
docs.shortcuts.customize.p2: You can also clear the shortcut entirely if you prefer to use the popup exclusively.
docs.shortcuts.popup.heading: Popup actions
docs.shortcuts.popup.p1: The same actions are available from the popup (click the Takt toolbar icon):
docs.shortcuts.popup.p2: Both the popup buttons and keyboard shortcuts apply to all open windows.
docs.sync.lead: Keep your rules, profiles, and sessions in sync across all your devices — by writing to a folder your cloud client already watches.
docs.sync.premiumCallout: Premium feature. File Sync requires a Premium subscription.
docs.sync.howItWorks.heading: How it works
docs.sync.howItWorks.p1: Takt writes four JSON files to a folder you choose. Your cloud desktop client — Google Drive, iCloud Drive, Dropbox, or any other — detects the new or changed files and uploads them. On your other devices, the client downloads the files, and you can pull them into Takt with Import from Folder.
docs.sync.howItWorks.p2: Takt handles the reading and writing. Your cloud client handles the transfer. No account linking or OAuth is required on Takt's side.
docs.sync.setup.heading: Setting up sync
docs.sync.setup.li1: Open the Takt Options page and go to the Settings tab.
docs.sync.setup.li2: Scroll to the File Sync section.
docs.sync.setup.li3: Click Choose Folder…. Chrome will open a folder picker.
docs.sync.setup.li4Intro: Navigate to a folder inside your cloud client's local directory — for example:
docs.sync.setup.li4: Click Select. Takt stores a reference to that folder.
docs.sync.setup.li5: Click Sync Now to write your data immediately.
docs.sync.setup.callout: Takt stores a handle to the folder, not the folder path as a string. Chrome remembers this handle across sessions, so you don't need to pick the folder again every time you open the browser — unless the folder is moved or deleted.
docs.sync.syncFiles.heading: What gets synced
docs.sync.syncFiles.intro: Takt writes four files to the chosen folder:
docs.sync.syncFiles.conclusion: Each file is written independently. If your cloud client supports per-file conflict resolution (like Google Drive), a change to rules on device A and profiles on device B will not conflict.
docs.sync.syncNow.heading: Sync Now
docs.sync.syncNow.p1: Click Sync Now to write all four files to the folder immediately. The Last synced timestamp updates when the write succeeds.
docs.sync.syncNow.p2: Use Sync Now after making a batch of changes you want to push to your other devices right away.
docs.sync.autoSync.heading: Auto-sync
docs.sync.autoSync.p1: Enable Auto-sync on every change to have Takt write to the folder automatically whenever your rules, profiles, or sessions change. There is a short delay (about 2 seconds) after the last change before the write happens, so rapid edits are batched into a single write.
docs.sync.autoSync.p2: Auto-sync runs silently in the background. If the write fails (for example, if the folder permission was lost after a browser restart), Takt surfaces a Re-authorize prompt the next time you open the Options page.
docs.sync.import.heading: Import from Folder
docs.sync.import.intro: Click Import from Folder to pull data into Takt from any folder on your disk — such as a cloud-synced folder on another device:
docs.sync.import.li2: Takt verifies that at least one expected file is present. If none are found, the import is cancelled with an error message.
docs.sync.import.li3: Each file is read, size-checked (5 MB limit), parsed, and validated for structure and security before any data is written.
docs.sync.import.li4: Valid data is merged into your current Takt data. Rules and profiles get fresh IDs to avoid collisions. Sessions each get a new ID.
docs.sync.import.p2: If a file is missing (for example, sessions.json doesn't exist yet), that data type is skipped without error.
docs.sync.import.callout: Don't import from your active sync folder. If you pick the same folder Takt is currently syncing to, Takt will read back the files it just wrote — this is harmless but pointless, and can create duplicate entries if you have made local changes since the last sync. Use Import from Folder to bring in data from a different device or a backup, not from the folder you sync to.
docs.sync.reauthorize.heading: Re-authorizing after permission loss
docs.sync.reauthorize.p1: In most cases Chrome remembers your folder permission across browser restarts and sync continues to work without any action. Occasionally permission can be lost — for example if the folder was moved or deleted, the extension was reinstalled, or Chrome's permission store was cleared.
docs.sync.reauthorize.blockquote: Folder access was lost. Click Re-authorize to restore sync.
docs.sync.reauthorize.p2: Click Re-authorize and Chrome will ask for permission to access the same folder again — no folder picker, just a one-click confirmation. Once granted, sync resumes automatically.
docs.sync.clear.heading: Clearing the sync folder
docs.sync.clear.p1: Click Clear next to the folder name to disconnect Takt from the sync folder. This removes Takt's reference to the folder and turns off auto-sync. It does not delete the files from your disk or from your cloud storage.
docs.sync.clear.p2: You can pick a different folder at any time by clicking Choose Folder… again.
docs.sync.limits.heading: Limits & behaviour
blog.compound.title: Match tabs by URL and title at the same time — Takt
blog.compound.metaDescription: Single-condition rules sort by domain. Compound AND/OR rules sort by context: authored PRs, draft docs, specific Slack channels. Here's how to set them up.
blog.compound.h1: Match tabs by URL and title at the same time
blog.compound.lede: Most tab rules look at one thing: the URL. That works for 80% of cases. The other 20% is where tabs share a domain but belong to different contexts, like Reddit.
blog.compound.p.1: Compound rules in Takt let you match on URL and title (or any combination, AND/OR). That covers the cases a single-condition rule can't.
blog.compound.h2.1: When a single condition isn't enough
blog.compound.p.3: A few real cases where URL alone doesn't tell you which group a tab belongs to:
blog.compound.li.single.1: GitHub/GitLab PRs/MRs you authored vs PRs/MRs you're reviewing. Same domain, same pull path but different mental context. Authoring is "in flight." Reviewing is "todo."
blog.compound.li.single.2: Google Docs drafts vs published docs. Same docs.google.com. The title is [DRAFT] X or X for example.
blog.compound.li.single.3: Linear issues assigned to you vs everyone else's. Same workspace URL. Title contains your name or doesn't.
blog.compound.li.single.4: Slack channels by team. Same app.slack.com. The title carries the channel name: #eng-backend - Acme Slack.
blog.compound.p.4: Each of these needs two pieces of info to sort correctly. Those are just examples that are highly software-development oriented, but you can apply the same principles to many other domains that are not necessarily related to a particular profession.
blog.compound.p.5: Open Takt, go to "Manage rules," click "+ New rule." Switch the condition block to AND (or OR, depending on what you need). Add multiple conditions.
blog.compound.p.6: Example with a "My open PRs" rule:
blog.compound.fig.1.alt: Takt GitHub example for pull tabs
blog.compound.fig.1.caption: Rule creation example to capture Takt GitHub example for pull tabs.
blog.compound.p.7: Now every PR with you in the title lands in red. Every other PR you happen to open lands in the regular Dev group via your domain rule.
blog.compound.h2.3: Three setups that pay off immediately
blog.compound.p.8: Anything you tag with [DRAFT] in the doc title gets pulled into Drafts. The rest stay in your normal Docs group.
blog.compound.h3.3: One Slack workspace, multiple project channels
blog.compound.p.9: Repeat per project. All your Acme Slack tabs route to the right project group based on which channel is open.
blog.compound.p.10: OR is useful when several distinct conditions all belong in the same bucket.
blog.compound.p.11: All your monitoring tabs in one group:
blog.compound.fig.2.alt: Takt example for a simple monitoring rule
blog.compound.fig.2.caption: Simple rule creation example to capture in a dedicated group tabs belonging to monitoring systems.
blog.compound.p.12: You could write three separate rules, but OR keeps it as one rule for the same group. Easier to manage and only counts as one rule against your quota.
blog.compound.p.13: Single-condition rules force a choice: either everything from a domain goes into one group, or you have to manually move the exceptions.
blog.compound.p.14: Compound rules let you describe context: "GitHub PRs where I'm the author" is a context, "Google Docs in draft state" is another one. The URL and title alone can't see it, but together they can.
blog.compound.li.notes.1: Title matching is also a Premium feature on its own, useful even without AND/OR.
blog.compound.li.notes.2: Specificity matters. A compound rule with three AND conditions runs against fewer tabs, so order from cheapest to most specific in your head when you write them.
blog.compound.li.notes.3: Why this is a Premium feature. Simple rules sort by where a tab lives. Compound rules sort by what it is: "PRs I authored", "docs in draft", "channel #project-alpha." That's a different category of capability, and a different category of work for the matcher (observing title changes, evaluating multiple conditions on every tab event). The free tier covers location-based sorting because that's enough for basic users. Compound matching is a more advanced capability that benefits power users.
blog.compound.cta.1: Add Takt to Chrome it's free, no account needed. Nothing leaves your browser.
blog.stopLosing.title: How to stop losing tabs when you have 90+ open — Takt
blog.stopLosing.metaDescription: A flat tab bar with 90 entries is unusable. Rules-based auto-grouping, keyboard search, and hibernation fix the problem at the source.
blog.stopLosing.h1: How to stop losing tabs when you have 90+ open
blog.stopLosing.lede: Open a few tabs for a task. Open more for a second task. At the end of the day, you have 90 tabs across two windows and no idea which one has the doc you were reading an hour ago.
blog.stopLosing.p.1: The tab bar is useless at that count: titles are truncated to a favicon, scrolling through them is slower than searching your history. Chrome's built-in tab search is several clicks away and doesn't solve the real issue: nothing is organized.
blog.stopLosing.p.2: That's where Takt helps you. It's a Chrome extension that auto-groups tabs by URL rules, finds any tab with a keyboard shortcut, and hibernates the ones you forgot about. Here's how it works in practice.
blog.stopLosing.p.3: Note: some of the features describes below are for the Premium plan.
blog.stopLosing.p.4: Having 90 tabs open is fine. Having 90 tabs in a flat, unsorted list is not.
blog.stopLosing.p.5: Chrome's native tab groups help, but creating them manually isn't sustained in the long run. You group a few tabs, forget for an hour, and the next 20 tabs land outside every group.
blog.stopLosing.p.6: The fix is making grouping automatic. Instead of dragging tabs into groups after the fact, you define rules once and let every new tab sort itself.
blog.stopLosing.h2.2: Create a rule in Takt, tabs sort themselves
blog.stopLosing.p.7: Open the Takt popup, go on the rules section, click "Manage rules" and then press "+ New rule" button. Three fields:
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.1: Rule name - what the tab group will be labeled: "Dev."
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.2: Conditions - the URL to match. For domain matching, just enter github.com.
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.3: Group name - what group will the rule belong to.
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.4: Color - pick from Chrome's eight group colors.
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.5: Auto-collapse group - select to auto-collapse a group when creating it.
blog.stopLosing.li.rule.6: Prevent duplicate tabs in this group - well, the name speaks for itself :).
blog.stopLosing.fig.1.caption: Takt rule creation.
blog.stopLosing.p.8: Save. Every open GitHub tab moves into a green "Dev" group immediately. Every future GitHub tab you open today, tomorrow, next week lands there automatically. No more manual overload.
blog.stopLosing.h2.3: What a practical setup looks like
blog.stopLosing.p.9: Most people's tab habits fall into four or five buckets. Here's a setup that takes about a minute to do:
blog.stopLosing.p.10: Four rules. Your tab bar goes from 90 grey favicons to four color-coded groups. Click "Group All" in the Takt popup (or use the keyboard shortcut Alt/Option+Shift+G) and every open tab that matches a rule sorts itself in one pass.
blog.stopLosing.p.11: Organization is half the problem. The other half is retrieval: jumping to a specific tab fast.
blog.stopLosing.p.12: Press Alt/Option+Shift+Space anywhere in Chrome. Takt opens a fuzzy-search overlay, type a few letters of any tab's title or URL and jump directly to it. No scrolling, no group scanning, just a quick access.
blog.stopLosing.fig.2.caption: Takt Quick Search.
blog.stopLosing.p.13: The search covers all open tabs, your browsing history, and hibernated tabs. At 90+ tabs, this is consistently faster than any visual scan.
blog.stopLosing.p.14: Tabs you forgot about still consume memory. A browser with 60 tabs can easily use 4-6 GB of RAM, and most of it goes to tabs you haven't looked at in hours.
blog.stopLosing.p.15: Takt's hibernation closes idle tabs while preserving their state: URL, title, group, scroll position. Memory is reclaimed the moment the tab closes. Wake any tab with one click and it returns exactly where you left it.
blog.stopLosing.p.16: Set an idle threshold and Takt hibernates tabs automatically. Your active workspace stays lean. Your idle tabs aren't costing you anything.
blog.stopLosing.fig.3.caption: Takt tab lifecycle.
blog.stopLosing.p.17: One minute of setup. Zero minutes of ongoing maintenance. Your tab bar is color-coded by context, any tab is a few keystrokes away, and idle tabs are asleep instead of eating RAM.
blog.stopLosing.cta.1: Add Takt to Chrome it's free, no account needed. Nothing leaves your browser.
